fuzzy_prd: We need a guided-tour engine that walks users through an unfamiliar interface one step at a time. The engine should maintain an ordered list of steps, track which step is currently showing, and let the developer navigate forward, backward, or jump to a specific step by its identifier. Steps that are not relevant in certain conditions should be automatically skipped during navigation — similar to how conditional visibility works in the existing modal module. The engine should fire lifecycle notifications so the rest of the application can react: when a step becomes visible, when the tour completes, and when it goes inactive. Advancing past the last step should automatically finish the tour. Jumping to a step identifier that doesn't exist should be a no-op with no side effects. The engine should also support adding new steps after the tour is built, looking up a step by its identifier, removing steps by identifier (and if the removed step was the one currently showing, automatically advance to the first remaining step). Underpinning all of this is a small event notification system where callbacks can be registered under named event keys, fired with an argument, removed individually or all at once, and optionally registered as one-shot (auto-removed after first firing). There is also a utility for normalizing a placement specification from a compact shorthand string into a structured object, and one for expanding space-separated option strings into keyed objects.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: export class Tour extends Evented { constructor(options={}); addStep(arg1, arg2); show(key, forward=true); start(); next(); back(); cancel(); complete(); hide(); isActive(); getCurrentStep(); getById(id); removeStep(name); }

trigger_keywords: what output format does a show notification use
how is the show event reported in the output
what does the show line look like when there is no previous step
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A show notification is printed as 'show <current_id> previous=<previous_id>' where previous_id is 'none' when there is no previous step (e.g., at tour start). Each show line ends with a newline.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid placement sides for attachTo
which placement values are accepted in the attach target
what placements can be used in step attach target
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The valid placement sides are: 'auto', 'top', 'left', 'right', 'bottom'. Each may optionally be suffixed with '-start' or '-end' (e.g., 'top-start', 'bottom-end'). Any other trailing token causes the specification to be rejected and output 'none'.

trigger_keywords: what happens when advancing past the last step
what lifecycle events fire when the tour completes
what is the exact completion sequence
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When advancing past the last step, the tour fires a 'complete' notification (output: 'complete'), then an 'inactive' notification (output: 'inactive'), and becomes inactive. isActive() returns false afterwards.

trigger_keywords: what happens when you remove the currently shown step
what if removeStep is called on the active step
what step shows after removing the current step
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the currently-shown step is removed and other steps remain, the tour automatically shows the first remaining step. This fires a show notification with the new step as current and the removed step as previous.

trigger_keywords: what does trigger output when no callbacks are registered
how is an empty trigger event reported
what output for a fired event with no listeners
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Firing an event with no registered callbacks outputs 'trigger <event>: (none)'. This is not an error.

trigger_keywords: what does the shorthand function return for undefined input
what happens when the value is absent/undefined in shorthand
how is undefined distinguished from null in shorthand
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the 'value' key is absent (undefined) the shorthand function returns undefined, reported as the single line 'undefined'. When value is explicitly null, it returns null, reported as 'null'. These are two distinct cases.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how conditional visibility works in the existing modal module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The 'showOn' property on a step is a boolean (or function returning boolean); when it evaluates to false at navigation time, the step is bypassed. This mirrors the conditional rendering logic in src/js/modal.js.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that detail. Please refer to the start.md specification and the public test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for authoritative requirements.

feature1_event_system.json.description: A reusable publish/subscribe notifier. Callers register named callbacks against string event keys, then fire an event to invoke every callback currently registered for that key, in registration order, forwarding the event's extra argument to each callback. A one-shot registration runs at most once and is then automatically discarded. Unregistering by event key alone removes every callback for that key; unregistering by event key plus a specific callback removes only that callback. Firing an event with no registered callbacks does nothing.

feature2_attach_target.json.description: Normalizes a step's attach-target specification into a canonical form with element selector and placement side.

feature3_shorthand.json.description: Expands a shorthand option value into a keyed object given an ordered list of field names.

feature4_1_navigation.json.description: Sequential forward/backward navigation across an ordered list of steps.
